frameworks.name: Filter
frameworks.description: Separating signal from noise. Filters reduce overwhelming information to a manageable subset. Filtering is always lossy — every filter trades completeness for tractability.
frameworks.when_to_use: When overwhelmed by information volume. Use for email triage, literature monitoring, notification management, any domain where inflow exceeds processing capacity.
key_concepts: Lossy reduction: every filter discards some signal along with noise
Multi-stage filtering: cascade coarse to fine for efficiency
Specificity vs. sensitivity tradeoff: tight filters miss noise but also signal
Context-dependent criteria: what counts as signal changes with the task
Bloom filters: probabilistic filters allowing false positives but never false negatives
frameworks.limitations: Filters create echo chambers when criteria are too narrow. Systematic blind spots. Over-filtering leads to surprise from black swan events.

frameworks.name: Network
frameworks.description: Interconnected nodes and edges revealing how structure shapes outcomes. Networks expose hidden patterns: who influences whom, where bottlenecks form, how information spreads.
frameworks.when_to_use: When relationships matter more than entities themselves. Use for influence patterns, key connectors, information flow, social structures, bottlenecks.
key_concepts: Degree: number of connections — high-degree nodes are hubs
Betweenness centrality: how often a node sits on shortest paths — gatekeepers
Clustering coefficient: how interconnected neighbors are — communities vs. bridges
Small-world property: most nodes reachable in few hops
Preferential attachment: rich-get-richer creates scale-free networks
frameworks.limitations: Assumes relationships are the primary driver, may miss individual attributes. Computationally expensive for large graphs. Edge definitions subjective.

frameworks.name: Threshold
frameworks.description: Actions trigger only when a variable crosses a critical value. Thresholds create discontinuities — small changes below threshold have no effect, crossing it triggers full response. Explains tipping points and sudden regime shifts.
frameworks.when_to_use: When outcomes are discontinuous. Use for tipping points in behavior, when to escalate vs. monitor, why gradual deterioration leads to sudden collapse.
key_concepts: Critical mass: collective action requires minimum participation level
Hysteresis: trigger threshold differs from reversal threshold
Granovetter's model: each person has a different threshold for joining
Multi-threshold systems: cascading thresholds create chain reactions
Threshold setting as optimization: too low = false alarms, too high = missed events
frameworks.limitations: Real-world thresholds are often fuzzy. Multiple interacting thresholds create unpredictable dynamics. Binary by nature — doesn't capture gradual degradation.

frameworks.name: Complementarity
frameworks.description: Combining different types produces more than the sum of parts. Complementarity explains why diverse teams outperform homogeneous ones, why cognitive diversity trumps ability, why multiple models beat any single model.
frameworks.when_to_use: When combining different perspectives/skills/approaches could yield better results than optimizing any single one. Use for team composition, tool selection, model ensembling.
key_concepts: Cognitive diversity: different tools produce different blind spots — combining reduces total
One-model trap: single framework creates systematic errors in uncovered dimensions
Complementarity vs. substitution: some things replace, others amplify — know which
Flat vs. peaked landscapes: complementarity matters most with multiple local optima
Many Model Thinker: wisdom comes from knowing which model to apply when
frameworks.limitations: Requires integration overhead. Doesn't apply when one approach strictly dominates. Coordination costs can exceed benefits.

frameworks.name: Signaling
frameworks.description: Actions convey hidden information. Signaling occurs when one party knows something another doesn't and takes costly action to reveal it. Costliness is essential — cheap signals are faked, costly ones are credible.
frameworks.when_to_use: When there's asymmetric information and you need to distinguish genuine quality from cheap talk. Use for understanding credentials, quality signals, communicating commitment.
key_concepts: Costly signaling: credible only when expensive enough that low-quality types wouldn't send
Separating equilibrium: high and low types choose different signals, revealing type
Pooling equilibrium: both types send same signal, no information revealed
Signal inflation: when everyone signals, credible signaling cost rises
Honest signals vs. cheap talk: words are cheap; actions and sacrifices are honest
frameworks.limitations: Assumes rational actors who correctly interpret signals. Signals can be misread or manipulated. Cost of signaling can be socially wasteful.

frameworks.name: Game Theory
frameworks.source: The Model Thinker / von Neumann & Morgenstern
frameworks.description: Strategic interaction where outcomes depend on what others choose. Reveals when cooperation emerges naturally, when conflict is inevitable, how payoff structure shapes behavior.
frameworks.when_to_use: When your best choice depends on what others choose. Use for negotiation, cooperation/failure analysis, incentive design, predicting responses to actions.
key_concepts: Nash equilibrium: no player benefits from unilaterally changing strategy
Prisoner's dilemma: individual rationality → collective irrationality — cooperation needs mechanism design
Repeated games: iteration enables cooperation via reputation and reciprocity (tit-for-tat)
Mechanism design: structuring payoffs so self-interest produces desired outcomes
Zero-sum vs. positive-sum: zero-sum = one's gain is another's loss; positive-sum = cooperation creates surplus
frameworks.limitations: Assumes rational payoff-maximizing actors — people often aren't rational. Requires knowing others' payoffs. Multiple equilibria make prediction difficult.

frameworks.name: Markov Process
frameworks.description: Next state depends only on current state, not on history. Captures path-independent dynamics: given where you are now, the past doesn't matter for predicting the future.
frameworks.when_to_use: When predicting transitions and current state captures all relevant information. Use for communication patterns, habit state transitions, workflow progression.
key_concepts: Memorylessness: P(next | current, past) = P(next | current)
Transition matrix: probabilities of moving between states
Stationary distribution: long-run probabilities converge regardless of starting state
Ergodicity: every state eventually reachable from every other
Mixing time: how quickly the process 'forgets' its initial state
frameworks.limitations: Markov assumption is strong — many real processes have long memory. Hidden states can violate the property. Ignores path-dependent effects.

frameworks.name: Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA)
frameworks.source: The Model Thinker / Blei, Ng & Jordan (2003)
frameworks.description: Discovers hidden thematic structure in document collections. Each document is a mixture of topics, each topic is a distribution over words. Inverting the generative process reveals latent themes without labeling.
frameworks.when_to_use: When discovering hidden structure in large text collections without manual labeling. Use for email categorization, conversation theme extraction, document clustering.
key_concepts: Latent topics: unobserved themes generating observed word distributions
Document-topic distribution: each document is a mixture of multiple topics
Topic-word distribution: each topic assigns different probabilities to words
Dirichlet prior: controls sparsity — topics per document, words per topic
Variational inference: approximate posterior computation for practical scaling
frameworks.limitations: Assumes bag-of-words (no word order), losing syntax. Topic interpretability requires human judgment. Choosing number of topics is more art than science.

frameworks.name: Diffusion
frameworks.description: How things spread through populations — ideas, behaviors, diseases, innovations. Reveals why some things go viral while others fizzle, how network structure affects spread speed.
frameworks.when_to_use: When understanding how something spreads through a population. Use for predicting habit adoption, information propagation, disease spread modeling, intervention design.
key_concepts: SIR model: Susceptible → Infected → Recovered — classic epidemic framework
R₀: average secondary infections per case — R₀ > 1 means epidemic
Bass diffusion: innovators (independent) + imitators (social influence)
Network structure: hubs accelerate, bottlenecks slow spread
Critical mass: diffusion fails if initial adopters are too few or poorly connected
frameworks.limitations: Simple forms assume homogeneous mixing — real networks have structure. Often ignores individual heterogeneity. Behavioral diffusion is messier than biological.

frameworks.name: Topic Model
frameworks.description: Discovers latent thematic structure in data. Beyond LDA: hierarchical variants, dynamic topic models tracking themes over time, correlated topic models capturing relationships between themes.
frameworks.when_to_use: When organizing large unstructured data by discovering hidden categories. Use for research literature organization, email archive analysis, meeting note clustering.
key_concepts: Latent structure: observed data is surface manifestation of deeper thematic organization
Dimensionality reduction: thousands of documents → dozens of topics
Dynamic topics: themes evolve over time — tracking conceptual drift
Hierarchical topics: themes nest within broader themes
Perplexity: measuring how well model predicts unseen data — model selection criterion
frameworks.limitations: Requires sufficient data volume. Topic interpretability is subjective. Struggles with short texts.

frameworks.name: Search
frameworks.description: Finding the best option in a space of possibilities. Reveals when to explore vs. exploit, how search space structure affects difficulty, why order matters.
frameworks.when_to_use: When finding the best option among many and can't evaluate them all. Use for literature review strategy, job search, any selection problem where evaluation cost requires strategy.
key_concepts: Explore-exploit tradeoff: gathering vs. using information — the fundamental search dilemma
Search space structure: smooth = gradient ascent; rugged = exploration needed
Optimal stopping: secretary problem (1/e ≈ 37% rule) for when to commit
Multi-armed bandit: balancing exploration of unknown with exploitation of known good
Information cascades: early results influence later searchers, creating herding
frameworks.limitations: Assumes you can evaluate options — evaluation itself may be costly or noisy. Often assumes stationary search space. Real spaces may be adversarial.

frameworks.name: Recommendation
frameworks.description: Predicting what someone will value based on similar people (collaborative filtering) or similar items (content-based). Reveals how preferences cluster, why filter bubbles form.
frameworks.when_to_use: When predicting preferences or suggesting options from a large catalog. Use for literature recommendation, content curation, skill suggestion, personalization at scale.
key_concepts: Collaborative filtering: 'people like you also liked...' — leveraging similar users
Content-based filtering: 'things like what you liked' — matching attributes to profiles
Cold start problem: new users/items have no preference data — hybrid approaches help
Filter bubble: over-optimizing relevance creates echo chambers — need serendipity
Explore-exploit in recommendation: known-good vs. potentially-better items
frameworks.limitations: Creates feedback loops — past recommendations shape future preferences. Struggles with novelty and diversity. Popularity bias amplifies already-popular items.

frameworks.name: Sigmoid / S-Curve
frameworks.description: S-shaped growth: slow start, rapid acceleration, then saturation. Same mechanism producing acceleration produces deceleration — success breeds saturation.
frameworks.when_to_use: When growth has natural limits and follows S-shaped trajectory. Use for predicting adoption curves, skill development plateaus, market saturation timing.
key_concepts: Three phases: lag, acceleration, saturation
Carrying capacity: the ceiling causing deceleration — every S-curve has one
Inflection point: where acceleration peaks and deceleration begins
Logistic growth: dP/dt = rP(1 - P/K) — growth proportional to remaining capacity
Stacked S-curves: new technologies create new curves before old ones saturate
frameworks.limitations: Real S-curves are noisy — inflection point only visible in retrospect. Multiple interacting curves create complex dynamics.

frameworks.name: Feedback Loop
frameworks.description: Outputs circle back as inputs, creating self-reinforcing or self-correcting dynamics. Positive feedback amplifies deviations, negative feedback dampens them. Most interesting behavior emerges from feedback loops.
frameworks.when_to_use: When system behavior is driven by circular causality — A affects B which affects A. Use for habit formation, health management, organizational dynamics, any self-correcting or self-amplifying system.
key_concepts: Positive (reinforcing) feedback: amplifies deviations — virtuous cycles or vicious spirals
Negative (balancing) feedback: dampens deviations — homeostasis, regulation
Delay in feedback: lag between action and consequence causes oscillation
Loop dominance: which loops are strongest determines system behavior
Leverage points: places where small changes produce large effects (Meadows)
frameworks.limitations: Becomes intractable when many loops interact. Identifying 'right' loops is subjective. Delays make causal attribution difficult.

frameworks.name: Conservation
frameworks.description: Resources neither created nor destroyed, only transformed. Reveals hidden constraints — time, energy, attention, money are finite, every allocation has opportunity cost.
frameworks.when_to_use: When resources are finite and every choice has a hidden cost. Use for time management, energy budgeting, attention allocation, any 'you can't have it all' constraint.
key_concepts: Total resources are zero-sum — more here means less there
Opportunity cost: true cost of any choice is the best alternative foregone
Hidden conservation: when something seems free, look for invisible depletion
Budget constraints: feasible set is bounded — can't exceed the total
Thermodynamic analogy: energy can be transformed but not created — efficiency has limits
frameworks.limitations: Not everything is conserved — knowledge and relationships can grow. Can lead to scarcity mindset when abundance exists. Some resources are renewable.

frameworks.name: Oscillator
frameworks.description: Rhythmic, repeating patterns — cycles, waves, periodic behavior. Describes circadian rhythms, economic cycles, mood fluctuations. Oscillation is natural and often healthy — absence of rhythm signals dysfunction.
frameworks.when_to_use: When behavior follows cyclical patterns and timing matters. Use for energy rhythms, scheduling around natural cycles, predicting performance peaks and troughs.
key_concepts: Natural frequency: intrinsic rhythm — circadian (~24h), ultradian (~90min)
Entrainment: external cues synchronize internal oscillators
Phase: where you are in the cycle — different phase, different action needed
Amplitude: strength of oscillation — high = big swings, low = stability
Damping: oscillations that decay vs. sustained oscillations that persist
frameworks.limitations: Real oscillators are noisy and quasi-periodic. Multiple overlapping oscillators create interference patterns. Entrainment disrupted by irregular schedules.

frameworks.name: Discounted Cash Flow (DCF)
frameworks.description: Values future cash flows by discounting to present value. A dollar today > a dollar tomorrow due to opportunity cost and uncertainty. Extends beyond finance — any future benefit can be discounted.
frameworks.when_to_use: When comparing costs/benefits at different times. Use for investment decisions, evaluating present sacrifice for future benefit, comparing projects with different time horizons.
key_concepts: Present value: PV = FV / (1 + r)^t
Discount rate: captures opportunity cost and risk — higher rate = future counts less
NPV: sum of discounted cash flows — positive NPV means value creation
IRR: discount rate making NPV = 0 — breakeven cost of capital
Terminal value: value beyond forecast period — often dominates calculation
frameworks.limitations: Extremely sensitive to discount rate and terminal value assumptions. Assumes predictable cash flows. Long-term forecasts are speculation in mathematical dress.

frameworks.name: Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)
frameworks.description: Relates expected return to systematic risk. Only non-diversifiable risk earns a premium — diversifiable risk is free to eliminate. Reveals why diversification is powerful.
frameworks.when_to_use: When evaluating whether expected return compensates for risk. Use for portfolio construction, understanding diversification, comparing risk-adjusted returns.
key_concepts: Beta (β): sensitivity to market — measures systematic risk
Security market line: expected return = risk-free rate + β × market risk premium
Diversification: idiosyncratic risk cancels in portfolios — only systematic remains
Alpha: return above CAPM prediction — measure of skill or mispricing
Risk-free rate: baseline return without risk — opportunity cost of risky investment
frameworks.limitations: Assumes efficient markets and rational investors — both often violated. Beta from history may not predict future. Single-factor misses other risk dimensions.

frameworks.name: Monte Carlo Simulation
frameworks.description: Uses random sampling to estimate outcomes under uncertainty. Runs thousands of plausible scenarios and counts what happens. Reveals full distribution of outcomes, exposing tail risks deterministic models miss.
frameworks.when_to_use: When uncertainty is deep and analytical solutions intractable. Use for financial planning, project timeline estimation, risk analysis, any decision where outcome range matters more than expected value.
key_concepts: Random sampling: draw from probability distributions for plausible scenarios
Law of large numbers: more samples → more accurate — convergence to true distribution
Confidence intervals: reveals full range, not just the mean
Tail risk: rare catastrophic outcomes that deterministic models miss
Variance reduction: techniques improving accuracy with fewer samples
frameworks.limitations: Only as good as input distributions — garbage in, garbage out. Computationally expensive for high dimensions. Rare events may be undersampled.

frameworks.name: Satisficing
frameworks.description: Choose the first option meeting a threshold of acceptability, rather than exhaustively searching for optimal. Search is costly, information incomplete, cognitive resources finite. Good enough now often beats perfect later.
frameworks.when_to_use: When cost of finding optimal exceeds benefit of optimality over adequacy. Use for decisions under time pressure, numerous options, decision fatigue, any 'good enough' situation.
key_concepts: Aspiration level: threshold defining 'good enough' — adjustable based on experience
Bounded rationality: can't optimize because information and computation are limited
Satisficing vs. maximizing: satisficers stop at first acceptable, maximizers search exhaustively
Adaptive aspiration: raise bar after success, lower after failure
Paradox of choice: more options worsen outcomes when maximizing, not when satisficing
frameworks.limitations: Can produce suboptimal choices when first acceptable option is far from optimal. Aspiration levels may be miscalibrated. Doesn't work well when options are irreversible.

frameworks.name: Multi-Objective Optimization
frameworks.description: Making decisions when multiple objectives conflict and can't be simultaneously optimized. Reveals tradeoffs, Pareto frontiers, why 'best' is undefined with multiple criteria.
frameworks.when_to_use: When competing objectives can't all be maximized. Use for career decisions, resource allocation, any situation where optimizing one dimension degrades another.
key_concepts: Pareto frontier: solutions where no objective can improve without another worsening
Tradeoff curves: visualizing how improving one objective requires sacrificing another
Scalarization: converting multiple objectives to one via weighted sum — weights are subjective
Constraint method: optimize one objective while constraining others to minimums
Dominance: A dominates B if better on all objectives — dominated solutions never optimal
frameworks.limitations: Choosing among Pareto-optimal solutions requires value judgments model can't make. Scalarization weights are arbitrary. Pareto frontier can be enormous.

frameworks.name: Value Function
frameworks.source: The Model Thinker / Prospect Theory (Kahneman & Tversky)
frameworks.description: How people subjectively value outcomes. S-shaped: concave for gains, convex for losses, steeper for losses than gains (loss aversion). Explains why losses loom larger than gains and framing effects occur.
frameworks.when_to_use: When understanding how people perceive and choose among risky options. Use for explaining risk-aversion for gains but risk-seeking for losses, framing effects, incentive design.
key_concepts: Loss aversion: losses hurt ~2x more than equivalent gains feel good
Reference dependence: value measured relative to reference point, not absolute
Diminishing sensitivity: $100→$200 feels larger than $1100→$1200
Endowment effect: owning raises subjective value — giving up feels like a loss
Framing effects: same outcome as gain vs. loss produces different choices
frameworks.limitations: Descriptive not prescriptive — describes how people do choose, not how they should. Reference points are context-dependent. 2x loss aversion varies across domains.

frameworks.name: Path Dependency
frameworks.description: Outcomes depend on sequence of events that led to them, not just current conditions. History matters: early random events can lock in outcomes regardless of later optimization.
frameworks.when_to_use: When early choices/events have outsized influence on long-term outcomes. Use for technological lock-in, career trajectory effects, first-mover advantage.
key_concepts: Increasing returns: early advantages compound — success breeds more success
Lock-in: once a path is chosen, switching costs make alternatives impractical
QWERTY effect: suboptimal standards persist because switching costs exceed efficiency gains
Critical junctures: moments where small events have large, irreversible consequences
Multiple equilibria: different paths lead to different stable states
frameworks.limitations: Hard to distinguish from structural constraints ex post. Not all history matters. Can be post-hoc rationalization rather than predictive.

frameworks.name: Redundancy
frameworks.description: Duplicate components provide fault tolerance. If any single component can fail, backups prevent system failure. Trades efficiency for resilience — wasteful until essential.
frameworks.when_to_use: When system failure is costly and components unreliable. Use for infrastructure design, data backup, service architecture, any domain where failure cost exceeds duplication cost.
key_concepts: N+1 redundancy: one more than minimum — survives single failure
N+2 redundancy: survives two simultaneous failures
Active vs. passive: active share load; passive wait as hot spares
Common-mode failure: same cause takes out all copies simultaneously
Reliability-cost tradeoff: each additional component improves reliability less than the last
frameworks.limitations: Adds complexity, cost, maintenance burden. Common-mode failures defeat redundancy. Over-redundancy creates complacency.

frameworks.name: Reliability
frameworks.description: Quantifying and improving probability that a system performs its function under stated conditions for a stated period. Connects component failure rates to system-level outcomes.
frameworks.when_to_use: When understanding or improving likelihood of system success over time. Use for service uptime, infrastructure design, SLA design.
key_concepts: Series systems: reliability = product of component reliabilities — one failure kills chain
Parallel systems: reliability = 1 - product of failure probabilities — survives single failures
MTBF: mean time between failures — average uptime, higher is better
MTTR: mean time to repair — average downtime, lower is better
Availability = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR): probability system is working when needed
frameworks.limitations: Assumes independent component failures — often doesn't hold. MTBF misunderstood as 'time until failure'. Aging effects complicate constant failure rate assumptions.

frameworks.name: Cascade
frameworks.description: One event triggers a chain of similar events, creating cascading failures or successes. Explains systemic risk — interconnections allow local failures to propagate globally.
frameworks.when_to_use: When failures/successes can propagate through interconnected systems. Use for systemic risk, diversification strategy, contagion analysis, circuit breaker design.
key_concepts: Contagion: spread of failure from one component to connected components
Circuit breakers: mechanisms stopping propagation — firebreaks, trading halts
Systemic risk: individual failures threaten entire system — 'too connected to fail'
Cascade vs. independent failure: cascades produce correlated failures overwhelming redundancy
Percolation threshold: connectivity level above which cascades become possible
frameworks.limitations: Sensitive to network structure assumptions. Predicting specific cascading failures is extremely difficult. Circuit breakers may stop legitimate propagation.

frameworks.name: Sandpile / Self-Organized Criticality
frameworks.description: Systems naturally evolve to critical state where any perturbation can produce events of any size — including catastrophic. Explains power-law distributions in earthquakes, market crashes, infrastructure failures.
frameworks.when_to_use: When small perturbations occasionally produce outsized consequences and power-law distributions appear. Use for understanding why catastrophic failures are inevitable, why resilience matters more than prevention.
key_concepts: Self-organized criticality: systems naturally evolve to critical state without external tuning
Power-law distributions: event sizes follow scale-free pattern — no characteristic scale
Avalanche sizes: most perturbations produce small avalanches, rare ones can be catastrophic
Critical state: always near tipping point — stability is illusory
Prevention vs. resilience: preventing all failures impossible; design for graceful failure
frameworks.limitations: Not all systems exhibit self-organized criticality — requires statistical evidence. Descriptive not predictive. Intervention points unclear.

frameworks.name: Emergence
frameworks.description: Macro-level patterns arise from micro-level interactions that don't contain the pattern themselves. Wetness isn't a property of any single water molecule, consciousness isn't in any single neuron.
frameworks.when_to_use: When system-level behavior can't be understood by studying components in isolation. Use for organizational culture, market dynamics, ecosystem behavior.
key_concepts: Weak emergence: macro patterns surprising but derivable from micro rules (in principle)
Strong emergence: macro patterns fundamentally irreducible to micro rules (controversial)
Self-organization: order arises without central control — from local interactions
Feedback between levels: macro patterns shape micro behavior reinforcing macro patterns
Reductionism limit: understanding parts doesn't guarantee understanding the whole
frameworks.limitations: Often invoked as explanation when it's really description. Can become catch-all that obscures rather than illuminates. Weak vs. strong emergence is philosophically contested.

frameworks.name: Agent-Based Model
frameworks.description: Autonomous agents follow simple rules and interact, producing complex emergent behavior. Reveals how macro patterns arise from micro rules, why heterogeneous agents produce different outcomes than representative agents.
frameworks.when_to_use: When understanding how individual-level rules produce system-level outcomes, especially with heterogeneity, local interactions, and adaptation. Use for market dynamics, organizational behavior.
key_concepts: Heterogeneous agents: individuals differ in rules, preferences, initial conditions
Local interactions: agents interact with neighbors — spatial structure matters
Simple rules, complex outcomes: Schelling's model — mild preferences produce extreme segregation
Path dependence: random seed matters — different runs produce different histories
Validation challenge: many parameterizations can produce same macro outcome
frameworks.limitations: Computationally expensive and difficult to validate. Equifinality — many parameterizations produce same outcome. Better for hypotheses than precise predictions.

frameworks.name: Heuristic
frameworks.description: Simple rules that perform well in specific environments. Not irrational shortcuts — adaptive strategies exploiting environmental structure. Recognition heuristic, take-the-best, and tallying often outperform complex models by avoiding overfitting.
frameworks.when_to_use: When simple rules can exploit environmental structure and complex models risk overfitting. Use for fast decisions under uncertainty, sparse data, limited cognitive resources.
key_concepts: Recognition heuristic: choose recognized option over unrecognized
Take-the-best: consider cues in validity order, stop at first discriminating cue
Tallying: count favorable cues without weighting — often as good as weighted regression
Less-is-more: simple heuristics outperform complex models in noisy environments
Ecological rationality: heuristic performance depends on environment structure, not model complexity
frameworks.limitations: Fail when environment doesn't match assumptions. Can produce systematic biases. Knowing which heuristic fits which environment requires domain expertise.

frameworks.name: Fitness Landscape
frameworks.description: Solutions map to a landscape of peaks and valleys. Reveals why local optima trap search, why rugged landscapes require exploration, why highest peak isn't always reachable.
frameworks.when_to_use: When evaluating solutions where quality varies and local improvements can trap you. Use for career optimization, skill development, product design.
key_concepts: Local optima: peaks that aren't highest — gradient ascent gets stuck
Global optimum: highest peak — may be unreachable from current position
Rugged vs. smooth: many peaks require exploration; one peak allows hill-climbing
Kauffman's NK model: N components with K interdependencies — higher K = more rugged
Adaptive walk: incremental improvement — finds local peak but can't cross valleys
frameworks.limitations: High-dimensional and impossible to visualize. Landscape may change as you search (coevolution). Defining 'fitness' is subjective and domain-dependent.

frameworks.name: Control Theory
frameworks.description: Keeping systems at desired states despite disturbances. Mathematical framework for feedback control: measure output, compare to target, adjust input. PID controllers, gain tuning, stability analysis.
frameworks.when_to_use: When maintaining a system at target state despite perturbations. Use for habit maintenance, energy management, workflow optimization, any domain where regulation matters more than raw optimization.
key_concepts: PID controller: Proportional (current error), Integral (accumulated), Derivative (rate of change)
Gain: how aggressively controller responds — too high = oscillation, too low = sluggish
Stability: system returns to target after disturbance — Bode plots, Nyquist criteria
Feedforward: anticipate disturbances before they occur — proactive vs. reactive
Robustness: maintaining performance despite model uncertainty — H-infinity control
frameworks.limitations: Assumes linear or linearizable systems — real systems often aren't. Requires measurable outputs and adjustable inputs. Over-engineering control creates brittleness.

frameworks.name: Reinforcement Learning
frameworks.description: Agent learns optimal behavior through trial and error, receiving rewards and penalties. Reveals explore-exploit dilemma in purest form, why delayed rewards make learning hard, how credit assignment works.
frameworks.when_to_use: When learning from experience rather than labeled examples. Use for habit formation strategy, understanding why immediate feedback accelerates learning, designing reward structures.
key_concepts: Explore-exploit dilemma: try new actions vs. use known good ones
Temporal credit assignment: which past action caused current reward?
Delayed rewards: long gaps between action and outcome make learning harder
Policy vs. value: what to do (policy) vs. how good a state is (value)
Exploration bonuses: intrinsic motivation to visit novel states — curiosity-driven learning
frameworks.limitations: Struggles with sparse rewards — rare feedback means slow learning. Credit assignment in long sequences is extremely difficult. Reward hacking: agents find unexpected ways to maximize reward.

frameworks.name: Circadian Rhythm
frameworks.description: ~24-hour internal clock regulating sleep-wake cycles, hormone release, body temperature, cognitive performance. Endogenous but entrainable (synchronized by light, meals, social cues). Disruption causes measurable performance degradation.
frameworks.when_to_use: When timing of activities matters for performance, health, or safety. Use for scheduling demanding work during peak alertness, understanding why shift work is harmful, optimizing sleep and meal timing.
key_concepts: Suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN): master clock in hypothalamus entrained by light
Circadian alignment: performance peaks when activity matches internal clock phase
Phase response curve: light at different times advances or delays the clock
Social jetlag: misalignment between biological and social clocks — measurable cognitive cost
Chronotype: individual variation in peak timing — morning larks vs. night owls
frameworks.limitations: Individual chronotype variation is large — one-size-fits-all schedules are suboptimal. Light exposure timing is critical but often uncontrollable. Shift work creates chronic misalignment.

frameworks.name: Temporal Dynamics
frameworks.description: How systems change over time — dynamics, rates, and trajectories. Reveals why timing matters, why rates of change are as important as directions, why the path through time constrains future possibilities.
frameworks.when_to_use: When understanding how a system evolves and timing matters. Use for predicting when changes will occur, understanding rate-dependent effects, any domain where 'when' and 'how fast' matter.
key_concepts: Rate of change: how fast a variable moves — often more important than current level
Trajectory: the path through state space over time — history matters
Equilibrium dynamics: how quickly a system reaches (or oscillates around) steady state
Time constants: characteristic timescales — fast vs. slow dynamics
Non-stationarity: when the rules themselves change over time — the model must adapt
frameworks.limitations: Requires knowing timescales, which are often uncertain. Non-stationarity makes historical data unreliable. Multiple overlapping timescales create complex dynamics.

frameworks.name: Optimization
frameworks.description: Finding the best solution from a set of alternatives subject to constraints. Reveals tradeoffs between objectives, why constraints shape solutions, why 'optimal' depends critically on what you're optimizing.
frameworks.when_to_use: When choosing the best option from many alternatives. Use for resource allocation, scheduling, portfolio construction, any domain where you can define an objective and constraints.
key_concepts: Objective function: what you're maximizing or minimizing — defines 'best'
Constraints: what must be satisfied — defines the feasible set
Convex vs. non-convex: convex has single global optimum; non-convex has many local optima
Gradient-based methods: follow the slope — efficient for smooth landscapes
Constraint relaxation: loosening constraints reveals which ones are binding
frameworks.limitations: Requires defining objective function and constraints — both subjective. Local optima trap gradient methods. Real problems are often non-convex. Optimization can overfit to specified objective.

frameworks.name: Centrality
frameworks.description: Measures of node importance within a network. Different measures capture different notions of importance: most connections (degree), controls information flow (betweenness), closest to everyone (closeness), connected to important nodes (eigenvector).
frameworks.when_to_use: When identifying key actors in a network. Use for finding influencers, bottlenecks, gatekeepers, vulnerable points in social, communication, or infrastructure networks.
key_concepts: Degree centrality: number of connections — who talks to most people
Betweenness centrality: on shortest paths between others — who controls information flow
Closeness centrality: average distance to all others — who can reach everyone fastest
Eigenvector centrality: connected to other central nodes — who knows important people
PageRank: eigenvector centrality with random jumps — Google's web page importance
frameworks.limitations: Different measures can identify different 'most important' nodes. Centrality is relative to network structure. Doesn't capture dynamics, only structure.

frameworks.name: Community Detection
frameworks.description: Identifying groups of nodes that are more densely connected internally than externally. Reveals modular structure of networks — subgroups, clusters, and boundaries not visible in the raw edge list.
frameworks.when_to_use: When discovering natural groupings in network data. Use for identifying social circles, organizational subgroups, topic clusters in citation networks.
key_concepts: Modularity: fraction of within-community edges minus expected fraction under random wiring
Louvain algorithm: greedy modularity optimization — fast and widely used
Overlapping communities: nodes can belong to multiple groups — most real communities overlap
Hierarchical communities: groups nest within larger groups — multiscale structure
Resolution limit: modularity optimization may miss small communities or merge large ones
frameworks.limitations: Modularity optimization has resolution limits. Ground truth for 'real' communities often doesn't exist. Different algorithms find different community structures.

frameworks.name: Meaning Mapping
frameworks.description: Mapping what gives life meaning across dimensions: purpose (direction), coherence (understanding), significance (mattering). Meaning isn't found — it's constructed from aligning actions with values and contributing beyond self.
frameworks.when_to_use: When evaluating whether activities, goals, or choices align with what gives life meaning. Use for career decisions, priority setting, understanding burnout as meaning deficit, any 'why am I doing this?' question.
key_concepts: Purpose: having a direction that gives life forward momentum — not a destination but a compass
Coherence: understanding how life events fit together — narrative identity
Significance: believing your existence matters to others — social belonging and impact
Values clarification: identifying what you genuinely care about vs. what you've been told to care about
Meaning vs. happiness: meaning involves challenge and sometimes suffering; happiness seeks comfort
frameworks.limitations: Meaning is subjective and culturally dependent — no universal formula. Can be used to justify suffering that should actually be alleviated. Over-intellectualizing meaning can undermine the felt experience.

frameworks.name: Purpose Alignment
frameworks.description: Aligning daily actions with a larger sense of purpose. Reveals misalignment between what you do and why you do it, provides framework for closing that gap through incremental adjustment rather than dramatic reinvention.
frameworks.when_to_use: When daily activities feel disconnected from what matters. Use for career alignment, identifying which tasks to delegate vs. own, understanding why burnout occurs despite 'success.'
key_concepts: Purpose statement: concise articulation of why your work matters — not a job description
Alignment audit: mapping time spent vs. purpose served — revealing misallocation
Micro-purpose: finding purpose in small moments, not just grand missions
Purpose decay: purposes shift over time — what felt meaningful at 25 may not at 40
Purpose portfolio: multiple purposes across life domains — diversification against meaning loss
frameworks.limitations: Purpose can be a luxury when basic needs aren't met. Over-optimizing for purpose can create anxiety about 'wasting time.' Purpose statements can become performative rather than authentic.

frameworks.name: Habit Loop
frameworks.description: Cue → Craving → Response → Reward. Explains why habits form, why they persist, and how to change them. Habits are automatic behavioral programs triggered by contextual cues, not conscious decisions.
frameworks.when_to_use: When forming new habits or breaking old ones. Use for any behavior change goal, understanding why willpower fails, designing environments that make desired behaviors automatic.
key_concepts: Cue: the trigger that initiates the behavior — time, place, emotional state, preceding action
Craving: the motivation behind the habit — every habit solves a problem you have
Response: the actual behavior — depends on friction and capability
Reward: what you get — satisfies the craving and teaches the brain to remember
Identity-based habits: 'I am a runner' beats 'I want to run' — change who you are, not what you do
frameworks.limitations: Habit loops are deeply ingrained and resistant to conscious override. Environmental cues are often invisible. Breaking habits requires replacing the routine, not just stopping it.

frameworks.name: Behavior Change
frameworks.description: Behavior = Motivation × Ability × Prompt (Fogg). Behavior occurs when all three are present simultaneously. Deconstructs 'why didn't I do X?' into specific fixable components.
frameworks.when_to_use: When trying to make a behavior happen or understanding why it isn't. Use for habit design, adoption of new tools/practices, understanding resistance to change, any 'I know I should but I don't' situation.
key_concepts: Motivation: pleasure/pain, hope/fear, social acceptance/rejection — must be sufficient
Ability: time, money, physical effort, brain cycles, social deviance, routine disruption — reduce friction
Prompt: cue, trigger, or reminder — must be present at the right moment
Fogg's maxim: 'Make it easy' — reducing ability barriers beats increasing motivation
Tiny habits: start with a version so small it requires almost no motivation — then grow
frameworks.limitations: Doesn't account for systemic barriers that make ability impossible regardless of motivation. Motivation is volatile — designs relying on sustained motivation fail. Cultural context affects all three components.

frameworks.name: Fogg's Behavior Model
frameworks.description: B=MAP: Behavior occurs when Motivation, Ability, and Prompt converge simultaneously. The action line divides possible from impossible: above the line, a prompt triggers behavior; below, it doesn't. Ability is easier to manipulate than motivation.
frameworks.when_to_use: When designing behavior change interventions. Use for habit formation, product adoption, health behavior change, any domain where 'just do it' fails.
key_concepts: B=MAP formula: behavior = motivation × ability × prompt — all three required
Action line: threshold above which prompts succeed — depends on motivation-ability combination
Ability anchors: time, money, physical effort, brain cycles, social deviance, non-routine
Motivation waves: motivation spikes and crashes — design for low-motivation moments
Celebration: immediately acknowledging success wires the brain — 'shine' moment creates habit
frameworks.limitations: Oversimplifies complex behaviors into three factors. Doesn't model habit formation over time — it's a single-event model. Cultural and systemic factors are backgrounded.

frameworks.name: Multi-Armed Bandit
frameworks.description: Balancing exploration of unknown options with exploitation of known good ones. Captures the fundamental dilemma of learning under uncertainty: every choice to explore is a choice not to exploit, and vice versa.
frameworks.when_to_use: When you must choose between known good options and potentially better unknown ones. Use for career exploration, A/B testing, research direction selection, any 'should I try something new or stick with what works?' decision.
key_concepts: Explore-exploit tradeoff: trying new options vs. using known best — the core dilemma
Regret minimization: optimal strategies minimize total regret over time
ε-greedy: explore with probability ε, exploit with probability 1-ε — simple but effective
Upper confidence bound: choose option with highest plausible value — optimistic in face of uncertainty
Thompson sampling: choose probabilistically based on posterior — Bayesian approach
frameworks.limitations: Assumes stationary reward distributions — real options may change over time. Doesn't model switching costs. Optimal strategy depends on time horizon, which is often unknown.

frameworks.name: Model of Models
frameworks.description: The meta-framework: use many models because no single model captures reality. Wisdom comes not from finding the one right model but from knowing which models apply when and combining their insights. Each model is a lens; many lenses reveal more than one.
frameworks.when_to_use: When facing complex problems that resist single-model analysis. Use as the default approach for any non-trivial decision — always ask 'what other models apply?' before committing to one framework.
key_concepts: Many-model thinking: use multiple models because reality is too complex for any one
Condorcet jury theorem: diverse independent models collectively outperform any individual
Model overlap and divergence: where models agree = confidence; where they disagree = investigation needed
Wisdom hierarchy: data → information → knowledge → wisdom — models accelerate the climb
The cat heuristic: 'all models are wrong, but some are useful' (Box) — seek usefulness, not truth
frameworks.limitations: Requires knowing multiple models — a knowledge investment. Integration of conflicting model outputs requires judgment. Can lead to analysis paralysis. Some problems genuinely have one best model.

frameworks.name: Priority Queue
frameworks.description: A data structure that always gives you the most important item next. Priority queues model how to allocate attention when everything is urgent but not everything is equally important. The key insight: processing order matters as much as processing speed.
frameworks.when_to_use: When you must decide what to do next and not everything can be done now. Use for task prioritization, triage, notification management, any domain where ordering decisions have consequence.
key_concepts: Priority inversion: when a low-priority task blocks a high-priority one — deadly in real-time systems
Dynamic priorities: priorities change as deadlines approach — urgency increases over time
Starvation: low-priority items never get processed — need aging mechanisms
Multi-level queues: different priority classes for different task types
Heap efficiency: always get the highest-priority item in O(log n) — fast prioritization at scale
frameworks.limitations: Priority assignment is subjective and often politicized. Static priorities miss urgency dynamics. Starvation of low-priority but important long-term tasks is a common failure mode.

frameworks.name: Attention Mechanism
frameworks.description: A computational mechanism that learns where to focus processing. Attention mechanisms learn to weight different inputs by relevance, enabling models to focus on what matters for the current task while ignoring distractions.
frameworks.when_to_use: When you need to dynamically focus on the most relevant information in a changing context. Use for understanding how to allocate cognitive resources, designing notification systems, any domain where focus must shift with context.
key_concepts: Query-key-value: what I want × what's available → how much to attend to each
Self-attention: elements attend to each other — capturing relationships without sequential processing
Multi-head attention: multiple attention patterns simultaneously — different aspects of relevance
Attention weights: soft selection — attending to everything but with different intensity
Sparse attention: most things are irrelevant — efficient attention focuses on few items
frameworks.limitations: Attention mechanisms can miss important but seemingly irrelevant information. They reflect training data biases. Computational cost scales quadratically with sequence length without optimizations.

frameworks.name: Information Theory
frameworks.description: Quantifying information content, transmission capacity, and the fundamental limits of communication. Information theory reveals how much signal can be extracted from noise, why compression has limits, and how entropy measures uncertainty.
frameworks.when_to_use: When quantifying information, uncertainty, or communication efficiency. Use for understanding data compression limits, measuring how informative a signal is, any domain where distinguishing signal from noise has mathematical limits.
key_concepts: Entropy: H(X) = -Σ p(x) log p(x) — measures uncertainty/surprise in a distribution
Mutual information: how much knowing X reduces uncertainty about Y — shared information
Channel capacity: maximum reliable communication rate — Shannon's theorem
Kolmogorov complexity: shortest program producing the data — measures intrinsic information content
Rate-distortion theory: how much information is preserved at a given compression level
frameworks.limitations: Information theory measures quantity, not meaning — high entropy doesn't mean high value. Assumes known probability distributions, which are often unavailable. Doesn't capture semantic content.
